1:
"After these things the word of the LORD came unto Abram in a vision, saying, Fear not, Abram: I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward." The witnesses note the word of the LORD assuring Abram, "I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward." On "the word of the LORD came unto Abram in a vision," the grammatical voices note the revelation. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the word of the LORD came unto Abram in a vision" — God revealed himself to Abram in a vision (the first mention of "the word of the LORD" coming to a man); the divine communication that opens the great covenant chapter; God drawing near to comfort and assure his servant after the war (ch. 14).

On "Fear not, Abram," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the comfort. Henry and Gill note that God said, "Fear not, Abram" — perhaps Abram feared the revenge of the defeated kings, or felt a reaction after the victory; God comforted him against fear; the assurance of the LORD to his servant; "fear not," the word of God to the believer.

On "I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the double promise: "I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward" — God promised to be Abram's shield (his protection and defense, against all enemies) and his exceeding great reward (his portion and recompense; far more than the spoil of Sodom he had refused, 14:23); God himself, not the wealth of Sodom, is Abram's reward; the LORD his protector and his portion; the believer's all in God. The verse displays the word of the LORD assuring Abram.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the revelation — "the word of the LORD came unto Abram in a vision," God revealing himself to comfort Abram (Keil, Ellicott); "Fear not, Abram," the comfort against fear (Henry, Gill); "I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward," God Abram's protection and portion, more than Sodom's spoil (14:23, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the assured Abram. The verse marks the word: of the LORD. The verse displays the fear: not. The verse displays the shield: God. The verse displays the reward: God himself. The assurance displays God as Abram's protector and portion. The verse displays the LORD: speaking. The verse displays the shield: and reward. The verse displays the Abram: comforted. The word of the LORD assuring Abram displays God as his all: fear not, I am thy shield and thy exceeding great reward — God himself, not the wealth of Sodom, Abram's protection and portion, the believer's all in God. The verse displays the assured Abram, who pleads his childlessness (v.2).
2:
"And Abram said, Lord GOD, what wilt thou give me, seeing I go childless, and the steward of my house is this Eliezer of Damascus?" The witnesses note Abram's plea over his childlessness. On "Lord GOD, what wilt thou give me, seeing I go childless," the grammatical voices note the complaint. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Lord GOD, what wilt thou give me, seeing I go childless" — Abram, in the freedom of faith, laid his trouble before God: what good were God's gifts while he remained childless, without the heir of promise? the patriarch's longing for the promised seed; the plea that, for all the promises, he had no child; the prayer of faith that lays hold of God's word and pleads the promise.

On "I go childless," the puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note the trial. Henry and Calvin note that Abram "go(es) childless" — still without a son, despite the promise of a seed (12:2, 7; 13:16); the long trial of his faith; the patriarch felt the delay; yet he brought his complaint to God in faith, not in unbelief; the believer pleading the promise in his trial.

On "the steward of my house is this Eliezer of Damascus," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the heir: "the steward of my house is this Eliezer of Damascus" — Eliezer, Abram's chief servant (steward), a Damascene, was, in default of a son, the prospective heir of Abram's house; the patriarch's household would pass to a servant, not a son; the want that pressed on Abram; the contrast with the promised seed; the occasion of God's renewed promise (vv.4-5). The verse displays Abram's plea over his childlessness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the complaint — "Lord GOD, what wilt thou give me, seeing I go childless," Abram pleading his childlessness before God (Keil, Gill); "I go childless," the trial of the delayed promise (12:2, Henry, Calvin); "the steward of my house is this Eliezer of Damascus," the servant the prospective heir, in default of a son (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the pleading Abram. The verse marks the plea: childless. The verse displays the heir: Eliezer. The verse displays the trial: of faith. The verse displays the promise: longed for. The plea displays Abram laying his want before God. The verse displays the Abram: pleading. The verse displays the childlessness: felt. The verse displays the seed: longed for. Abram's plea over his childlessness displays the prayer of faith in trial: Lord GOD, what wilt thou give me, seeing I go childless, and Eliezer is my heir? — the patriarch, longing for the promised seed, laying his trouble before God, pleading the promise in the trial of its delay. The verse displays the pleading Abram, who reckons his servant his heir (v.3).
3:
"And Abram said, Behold, to me thou hast given no seed: and, lo, one born in my house is mine heir." The brief verse notes Abram pressing his complaint that a servant must be his heir. The grammatical voices note the plea. Gill and Ellicott note that "Behold, to me thou hast given no seed: and, lo, one born in my house is mine heir" — Abram pressed his complaint further: God had given him no seed (no child of his own), so that one born in his house (a home-born servant, Eliezer) must be his heir; the patriarch's want of an heir of his own body; the renewed laying of his trouble before God.

On "to me thou hast given no seed," the reformed Calvin notes the trial. Calvin and Gill note that Abram said "to me thou hast given no seed" — for all the promises, he had as yet no child; the long delay of the promised seed; the trial of faith pressing on the patriarch; yet his complaint was the plea of faith, pressing the promise before God.

On "one born in my house is mine heir," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the heir: "one born in my house is mine heir" — in default of a son, Abram's home-born servant (Eliezer) would inherit; the patriarch's estate would pass to a servant; the want that occasioned God's express promise of a son of his own body (v.4); the contrast between the servant-heir and the promised seed. The verse displays Abram pressing his complaint.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the plea — "to me thou hast given no seed: and, lo, one born in my house is mine heir," Abram's complaint that a servant must be his heir (Gill, Ellicott); "to me thou hast given no seed," the trial of the delayed promise (Calvin, Gill); "one born in my house is mine heir," the servant-heir, in default of a son (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the pleading Abram. The verse marks the seed: none given. The verse displays the heir: a servant. The verse displays the trial: pressed. The verse displays the promise: awaited. The plea displays Abram pressing his want before God. The verse displays the Abram: complaining. The verse displays the seed: lacking. The verse displays the heir: a servant. Abram pressing his complaint that a servant must be his heir displays the trial of the promise: to me thou hast given no seed, and a home-born servant is my heir — the patriarch, lacking a son, pressing his want before God, the servant-heir setting off the promised seed to come. The verse displays the pleading Abram, answered by God's promise (v.4).
4:
"And, behold, the word of the LORD came unto him, saying, This shall not be thine heir; but he that shall come forth out of thine own bowels shall be thine heir." The witnesses note God's promise of a son of Abram's own body. On "the word of the LORD came unto him, saying, This shall not be thine heir," the grammatical voices note the answer. Gill and Calvin note that "the word of the LORD came unto him, saying, This shall not be thine heir" — God answered Abram's complaint: the servant (Eliezer) would not be his heir; God set aside the servant-heir; the LORD's word correcting Abram's fear and renewing the promise of a true seed.

On "he that shall come forth out of thine own bowels shall be thine heir," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the promise. Calvin and Gill note that "he that shall come forth out of thine own bowels shall be thine heir" — God promised Abram an heir of his own body, a son born to him; not a servant, but a true child; the express promise of a son (fulfilled in Isaac, ch. 21); the renewal and clarifying of the seed-promise; the patriarch assured of a child of his own.

On the promise, the grammatical-historical note the assurance: God's word that Abram's own son would be his heir answered the patriarch's plea (vv.2-3); the promise of a true seed, against the human impossibility (Abram old, Sarai barren); the assurance that God would give what nature could not; the promise that called for and strengthened Abram's faith (v.6). The verse displays God's promise of a son of Abram's own body.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the answer — "the word of the LORD came unto him, saying, This shall not be thine heir," God setting aside the servant-heir (Gill, Calvin); "he that shall come forth out of thine own bowels shall be thine heir," the promise of a son of Abram's body (Isaac, ch. 21, Calvin, Gill); the assurance against the human impossibility (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promised son. The verse marks the heir: not the servant. The verse displays the son: of Abram's body. The verse displays the promise: renewed. The verse displays the faith: strengthened. The promise displays God assuring Abram of a true seed. The verse displays the LORD: promising. The verse displays the heir: a son. The verse displays the seed: assured. God's promise of a son of Abram's own body displays the renewed seed-promise: not the servant, but he that comes from thine own bowels shall be thine heir — God assuring Abram of a true son (Isaac), against the human impossibility, the promise that strengthened his faith. The verse displays the promised son, with the stars of heaven (v.5).
5:
"And he brought him forth abroad, and said, Look now toward heaven, and tell the stars, if thou be able to number them: and he said unto him, So shall thy seed be." The witnesses note God showing Abram the stars as the measure of his seed. On "he brought him forth abroad, and said, Look now toward heaven, and tell the stars," the grammatical voices note the sign. Ellicott and Gill note that "he brought him forth abroad, and said, Look now toward heaven, and tell the stars, if thou be able to number them" — God brought Abram out (it was night, a vision, v.12) and bade him look at the stars and try to count them; the innumerable stars set before Abram as a visible measure of his promised seed; the sign that strengthened his faith.

On "tell the stars, if thou be able to number them," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the multitude. Clarke and Gill note that the stars are innumerable to man; as Abram could not number them, so his seed would be beyond numbering; the promise of an innumerable posterity (as the dust, 13:16, and now the stars); the vast multitude of his descendants, natural (Israel) and spiritual (all the faithful, Romans 4:18; Galatians 3:7, 29).

On "So shall thy seed be," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the promise: "So shall thy seed be" — as the stars, so numberless would Abram's seed be; the promise that against all human impossibility (the childless old man) God would give an innumerable posterity; the word that Abram believed (v.6); the seed of promise, as the stars of heaven, the children of faith. The verse displays God showing Abram the stars as the measure of his seed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sign — "Look now toward heaven, and tell the stars, if thou be able to number them," God setting the innumerable stars before Abram (Ellicott, Gill); "tell the stars," the seed beyond numbering, as the stars (13:16; Romans 4:18, Clarke, Gill); "So shall thy seed be," the promise of an innumerable posterity (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the starry promise. The verse marks the stars: innumerable. The verse displays the heaven: looked to. The verse displays the seed: as the stars. The verse displays the faith: strengthened. The sign displays Abram's seed measured by the stars. The verse displays the stars: shown. The verse displays the seed: countless. The verse displays the promise: given. God showing Abram the stars as the measure of his seed displays the promise of an innumerable posterity: look toward heaven and number the stars, so shall thy seed be — the childless patriarch shown the innumerable stars as the measure of his seed (Romans 4:18), the promise he would believe. The verse displays the starry promise, believed by Abram (v.6).
6:
"And he believed in the LORD; and he counted it to him for righteousness." The witnesses note Abram's faith counted to him for righteousness — the foundation text of justification by faith. On "he believed in the LORD," the grammatical voices note the faith. Gill and Calvin note that "he believed in the LORD" — Abram believed God's promise (of the seed, v.5, and so of the Messiah and all the covenant blessings); he rested his faith on God and his word; trusting God who quickens the dead and calls things that are not as though they were (Romans 4:17); the faith of the patriarch, the pattern of all believing.

On "he counted it to him for righteousness," interpreters expound the great doctrine. The reformed Calvin and the evangelical reading hold this is the foundation text of justification by faith: God "counted it (Abram's faith) to him for righteousness" — Abram's faith was reckoned, imputed, accounted to him for righteousness; not that faith is itself a meritorious work, but that, by faith, the righteousness of God (ultimately the righteousness of Christ) is imputed to the believer; Abram was justified, accounted righteous before God, not by works but by faith (Romans 4:3, 22; Galatians 3:6); the doctrine of justification by faith, apart from works, is here founded (Romans 4); faith the instrument, not the ground, of justification; the righteousness reckoned is God's gift, received by faith. A complementary reading (with James 2:23) notes that Abram's faith was a living, working faith, shown by his obedience; but the imputation is of faith for righteousness, the free reckoning of righteousness to the believing sinner.

On the imputation, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the doctrine: the verse teaches that righteousness (tsedakah, justification) is reckoned to faith; Abram, believing, was counted righteous; the great gospel doctrine, that the sinner is justified by faith, not by works (Romans 4); the pattern of the justification of all who believe, Jew and Gentile (Romans 4:11-12, 23-24); Abram the father of the faithful. The verse displays Abram's faith counted to him for righteousness.

The witnesses agree the substance: Abram believed God, and his faith was counted to him for righteousness — the foundation of justification by faith (Gill, Calvin, Clarke; Romans 4:3, 22; Galatians 3:6; James 2:23). The verse founds the doctrine: faith reckoned/imputed for righteousness — justification by faith apart from works (the evangelical/Reformed reading, Romans 4), faith the instrument by which God's (Christ's) righteousness is imputed, not faith as a meritorious work; with James noting the faith that justifies is a living, working faith. The verse displays the justification-by-faith doctrine. The verse marks the faith: Abram believed. The verse displays the righteousness: counted to him. The verse displays the doctrine: justification by faith. The verse displays the imputation: of righteousness. The reckoning displays the believing sinner accounted righteous. The verse displays the Abram: believing. The verse displays the righteousness: imputed. The verse displays the gospel: founded. Abram's faith counted to him for righteousness displays the foundation of justification by faith: he believed in the LORD, and it was counted to him for righteousness — Abram justified, accounted righteous, not by works but by faith (Romans 4:3, 22; Galatians 3:6), the righteousness of God imputed to the believer, the pattern of all who believe. The verse displays the justified Abram, assured of the land (v.7).
7:
"And he said unto him, I am the LORD that brought thee out of Ur of the Chaldees, to give thee this land to inherit it." The witnesses note God's declaration as the LORD who brought Abram out to give him the land. On "I am the LORD that brought thee out of Ur of the Chaldees," the grammatical voices note the self-revelation. Gill and Henry note that "I am the LORD that brought thee out of Ur of the Chaldees" — God declared himself the LORD (Jehovah) who had called and brought Abram out of Ur (11:31; 12:1; Acts 7:2-4); the God who began the work would complete it; the ground of the promise — the faithful God who had already led Abram thus far.

On "brought thee out of Ur of the Chaldees," the puritan Henry notes the calling. Henry and Gill note that God reminded Abram of his calling out of Ur — the God who brought him from his idolatrous homeland (Joshua 24:2) to the land of promise; the past mercy the pledge of the future; the faithful God who had begun would finish.

On "to give thee this land to inherit it," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the purpose: "to give thee this land to inherit it" — God's purpose in calling Abram was to give him the land (Canaan) for an inheritance; the land-promise renewed; the same God who promised the seed (v.5) now confirmed the land; the double promise of seed and land, the substance of the covenant; the assurance that Abram's seed would inherit the land. The verse displays God's declaration as the LORD who brought Abram out to give him the land.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the self-revelation — "I am the LORD that brought thee out of Ur of the Chaldees," God the faithful LORD who called Abram (12:1; Acts 7:2-4, Gill, Henry); "brought thee out of Ur," the calling from the idolatrous homeland (Joshua 24:2, Henry, Gill); "to give thee this land to inherit it," the land-promise renewed (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the declaring LORD. The verse marks the LORD: who called Abram. The verse displays the Ur: brought out. The verse displays the land: to inherit. The verse displays the purpose: the inheritance. The declaration displays God the faithful giver of the land. The verse displays the LORD: revealed. The verse displays the calling: recalled. The verse displays the land: promised. God's declaration as the LORD who brought Abram out to give him the land displays the faithful covenant God: I am the LORD that brought thee out of Ur to give thee this land — the God who called Abram from Ur now confirming the land-promise, the past mercy the pledge of the inheritance. The verse displays the declaring LORD, asked for a sign (v.8).
8:
"And he said, Lord GOD, whereby shall I know that I shall inherit it?" The brief verse notes Abram's request for a sign of the inheritance. The grammatical voices note the question. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Lord GOD, whereby shall I know that I shall inherit it?" — Abram asked for assurance, a sign or token, that he (his seed) would indeed inherit the land; the question of faith seeking confirmation, not of unbelief doubting the promise; the patriarch asking how he might know the promise sure; the request that God would answer by the covenant of the pieces (vv.9-17).

On "whereby shall I know," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the faith. Calvin and Gill note that Abram's question was not the doubt of unbelief (he believed, v.6) but the desire of faith for a confirming sign; like the believer who, resting on the promise, yet seeks the seal; God graciously condescended to give the assurance; the faith that seeks and receives confirmation.

On the request, the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the answer to come: Abram's request for a sign was answered by God's solemn covenant (vv.9-17) — the sacrifice, the divided pieces, and the passing of the smoking furnace and burning lamp between them; God confirmed the land-promise by covenant; the assurance Abram sought given in the most solemn form. The verse displays Abram's request for a sign of the inheritance.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the question — "Lord GOD, whereby shall I know that I shall inherit it?" Abram asking for assurance of the inheritance (Keil, Gill); the question of faith seeking confirmation, not of unbelief (Calvin, Gill); the request answered by the covenant (vv.9-17, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the asking Abram. The verse marks the question: whereby shall I know. The verse displays the inheritance: assurance sought. The verse displays the faith: seeking a sign. The verse displays the covenant: to come. The request displays Abram seeking confirmation of the promise. The verse displays the Abram: asking. The verse displays the sign: sought. The verse displays the assurance: to be given. Abram's request for a sign of the inheritance displays the faith that seeks confirmation: Lord GOD, whereby shall I know that I shall inherit it? — the patriarch, believing, yet seeking a confirming sign, which God would give in the solemn covenant of the pieces. The verse displays the asking Abram, bidden to take the sacrifice (v.9).
9:
"And he said unto him, Take me an heifer of three years old, and a she goat of three years old, and a ram of three years old, and a turtledove, and a young pigeon." The witnesses note God's command to take the covenant sacrifices. On "Take me an heifer of three years old... and a ram of three years old," the grammatical voices note the animals. Gill and Calvin note that "Take me an heifer of three years old, and a she goat of three years old, and a ram of three years old, and a turtledove, and a young pigeon" — God commanded Abram to take certain animals (a heifer, a she-goat, a ram, each three years old, and two birds) for the covenant sacrifice; the animals later used in the Levitical sacrifices; the materials of the solemn covenant God would make (vv.10, 17).

On the animals, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the sacrifice. Clarke and Gill note that the animals (the clean beasts and birds, fit for sacrifice) were to be prepared for a covenant ceremony; the ancient mode of making a covenant by sacrifice (cutting the animals and passing between the pieces); God's covenants with man made by sacrifice; the sacrificial animals prefiguring the great sacrifice.

On "three years old," the grammatical-historical note the age: "three years old" — the animals in their full strength and prime; fit for the solemn sacrifice; the careful specification marking the importance of the covenant; the materials prepared, to be divided (v.10) for the covenant of the pieces. The verse displays God's command to take the covenant sacrifices.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the animals — "Take me an heifer of three years old... and a ram of three years old, and a turtledove, and a young pigeon," the animals for the covenant sacrifice (Gill, Calvin); the sacrifice the mode of making the covenant (Clarke, Gill); "three years old," the animals in their prime, fit for sacrifice (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the commanded sacrifice. The verse marks the animals: heifer, goat, ram, birds. The verse displays the age: three years. The verse displays the covenant: by sacrifice. The verse displays the materials: prepared. The command displays God appointing the covenant sacrifice. The verse displays the animals: taken. The verse displays the sacrifice: prepared. The verse displays the covenant: to be made. God's command to take the covenant sacrifices displays the materials of the covenant: take a heifer, a she-goat, a ram, a turtledove, and a pigeon — the animals for the solemn covenant God would make by sacrifice, to be divided for the covenant of the pieces. The verse displays the commanded sacrifice, divided by Abram (v.10).
10:
"And he took unto him all these, and divided them in the midst, and laid each piece one against another: but the birds divided he not." The witnesses note Abram dividing the sacrifices for the covenant. On "he took unto him all these, and divided them in the midst," the grammatical voices note the dividing. Gill and Clarke note that "he took unto him all these, and divided them in the midst, and laid each piece one against another" — Abram took the animals, cut them in two (divided them down the middle), and laid the pieces opposite each other; the ancient rite of covenant-making, in which the parties passed between the divided pieces (Jeremiah 34:18-19); the preparation for the solemn covenant.

On "laid each piece one against another," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the rite. Calvin and Gill note that the pieces were laid opposite each other, leaving a path between; the manner of the covenant-rite, in which to pass between the pieces was to invoke the like fate (to be cut in pieces) on the breaker of the covenant; the solemn, self-imprecatory form of the ancient covenant; the path between the pieces through which the covenant-maker would pass (v.17).

On "the birds divided he not," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the birds: "but the birds divided he not" — the birds (the turtledove and pigeon) were not divided (perhaps as being small, or by custom, or as the doves in sacrifice were not split, Leviticus 1:17); the two birds laid whole, perhaps one on each side; the careful following of the rite; the sacrifice prepared for the covenant ceremony. The verse displays Abram dividing the sacrifices for the covenant.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the dividing — "he took unto him all these, and divided them in the midst, and laid each piece one against another," Abram cutting the animals and laying the pieces opposite (Jeremiah 34:18-19, Gill, Clarke); the rite of passing between the pieces (Calvin, Gill); "the birds divided he not," the birds laid whole (Leviticus 1:17, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the divided sacrifice. The verse marks the pieces: divided. The verse displays the path: between them. The verse displays the rite: of covenant. The verse displays the birds: not divided. The dividing displays Abram preparing the covenant-sacrifice. The verse displays the animals: cut. The verse displays the pieces: opposite. The verse displays the covenant: prepared. Abram dividing the sacrifices for the covenant displays the ancient covenant-rite: he divided the animals and laid the pieces opposite, but the birds he divided not — the preparation of the covenant-sacrifice, the path between the pieces through which God would pass (v.17), the solemn rite of covenant-making (Jeremiah 34:18-19). The verse displays the divided sacrifice, guarded from the fowls (v.11).
11:
"And when the fowls came down upon the carcases, Abram drove them away." The brief verse notes Abram driving away the birds of prey from the sacrifice. The grammatical voices note the act. Gill and Ellicott note that "when the fowls came down upon the carcases, Abram drove them away" — birds of prey (vultures, etc.) descended on the divided carcasses, and Abram drove them off; the patriarch guarded the sacrifice from defilement until God should act; the watchful care of Abram over the covenant-offering.

On "the fowls came down upon the carcases," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the symbol. Clarke and Gill note that the birds of prey coming down on the sacrifice have been variously taken — as a hindrance Abram drove off; and (by some) as a symbol — of the enemies of Abram's seed (the Egyptians, or the nations) who would seek to destroy them, or of Satan and the foes of the covenant; Abram's driving them away figuring the protection of the covenant-people; the trial of Abram's patience as he watched and waited.

On "Abram drove them away," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the vigilance: "Abram drove them away" — Abram, watching by the sacrifice, drove off the unclean birds; his patient vigilance through the day, awaiting God's covenant-sign (v.12, the sun going down); the faithfulness of the patriarch in guarding the offering; the believer's watchful patience as he waits on God. The verse displays Abram driving away the birds of prey.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the act — "when the fowls came down upon the carcases, Abram drove them away," Abram guarding the sacrifice from the birds of prey (Gill, Ellicott); the birds variously a hindrance or a symbol of the enemies of the seed (Clarke, Gill); "Abram drove them away," the patriarch's patient vigilance (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the watching Abram. The verse marks the fowls: came down. The verse displays the carcases: the sacrifice. The verse displays the driving: away. The verse displays the vigilance: of Abram. The act displays Abram guarding the covenant-offering. The verse displays the birds: driven off. The verse displays the sacrifice: guarded. The verse displays the patience: shown. Abram driving away the birds of prey displays his watchful vigilance over the sacrifice: when the fowls came down on the carcasses, Abram drove them away — the patriarch guarding the covenant-offering (the birds taken by some as the enemies of the seed), waiting patiently on God. The verse displays the watching Abram, falling into a deep sleep (v.12).
12:
"And when the sun was going down, a deep sleep fell upon Abram; and, lo, an horror of great darkness fell upon him." The witnesses note the deep sleep and the horror of great darkness that fell on Abram. On "when the sun was going down, a deep sleep fell upon Abram," the grammatical voices note the trance. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when the sun was going down, a deep sleep (tardemah) fell upon Abram" — at sunset, a deep, supernatural sleep (like that on Adam, 2:21) fell on Abram; the trance in which God would reveal the future and make the covenant; the divinely-induced sleep that prepared the patriarch for the revelation (vv.13-16) and the covenant-sign (v.17).

On "an horror of great darkness fell upon him," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the dread. Henry and Calvin note that "an horror of great darkness fell upon him" — a great dread and darkness came over Abram; the awe and terror of the divine presence; and (as the revelation would show, vv.13-16) a foreshadowing of the affliction and bondage of his seed in Egypt (the darkness figuring the coming trial); the solemn dread that accompanied the covenant-revelation.

On the horror of darkness, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the foreshadowing: the horror of great darkness foreshadowed the dark affliction of Abram's seed (the bondage in Egypt, v.13); the dread that came on the patriarch prefigured the trial of his descendants; yet the covenant (v.18) and the deliverance (v.14) would follow the darkness; the darkness before the dawn of the promise. The verse displays the deep sleep and the horror of great darkness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sleep — "when the sun was going down, a deep sleep fell upon Abram," the supernatural trance for the revelation (2:21, Keil, Gill); "an horror of great darkness fell upon him," the dread of the divine presence, foreshadowing the affliction (Henry, Calvin); the darkness prefiguring the bondage in Egypt (v.13, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the entranced Abram. The verse marks the sleep: deep. The verse displays the darkness: a horror. The verse displays the dread: divine. The verse displays the foreshadowing: of affliction. The trance displays Abram prepared for the covenant-revelation. The verse displays the Abram: asleep. The verse displays the darkness: fallen. The verse displays the dread: great. The deep sleep and the horror of great darkness display the solemn covenant-revelation: at sunset a deep sleep and a horror of great darkness fell on Abram — the supernatural trance in which God revealed the future, the darkness foreshadowing the affliction of his seed (v.13) before the deliverance and the covenant. The verse displays the entranced Abram, told of the bondage (v.13).
13:
"And he said unto Abram, Know of a surety that thy seed shall be a stranger in a land that is not theirs, and shall serve them; and they shall afflict them four hundred years;" The witnesses note the prophecy of the bondage and affliction of Abram's seed. On "Know of a surety that thy seed shall be a stranger in a land that is not theirs," the grammatical voices note the prophecy. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Know of a surety that thy seed shall be a stranger in a land that is not theirs, and shall serve them; and they shall afflict them" — God foretold that Abram's seed would be strangers and slaves in a foreign land (Egypt), serving and afflicted; the prophecy of the Egyptian bondage (Exodus 1; Acts 7:6); the future of the chosen seed revealed to Abram.

On "shall serve them; and they shall afflict them," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the affliction. Clarke and Ellicott note that the seed would "serve them, and they shall afflict them" — the harsh bondage and affliction of Israel in Egypt; the suffering of the chosen people before their deliverance; the trial appointed for the seed of promise.

On "four hundred years," the grammatical-historical note the period: "four hundred years" — the round number for the period of the sojourning and affliction (the four hundred years, or four hundred thirty, Exodus 12:40; Galatians 3:17, reckoned variously, from Abram's seed being strangers, or from the entry into Egypt); the appointed time of the bondage; the period after which the deliverance would come (v.14); the foretold duration of the affliction. The verse displays the prophecy of the bondage and affliction of Abram's seed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the prophecy — "Know of a surety that thy seed shall be a stranger in a land that is not theirs, and shall serve them," the foretold bondage in Egypt (Exodus 1; Acts 7:6, Keil, Gill); "they shall afflict them," the harsh affliction of Israel (Clarke, Ellicott); "four hundred years," the appointed period of the sojourn and affliction (Exodus 12:40; Galatians 3:17, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the foretold bondage. The verse marks the seed: strangers. The verse displays the bondage: serving. The verse displays the affliction: foretold. The verse displays the period: 400 years. The prophecy displays the future bondage of the seed revealed. The verse displays the seed: enslaved. The verse displays the affliction: appointed. The verse displays the deliverance: to come. The prophecy of the bondage and affliction of Abram's seed displays the foretold trial: thy seed shall be strangers and slaves, afflicted four hundred years — the Egyptian bondage (Acts 7:6) revealed to Abram, the appointed trial of the chosen seed before their deliverance. The verse displays the foretold bondage, with the promise of judgment and deliverance (v.14).
14:
"And also that nation, whom they shall serve, will I judge: and afterward shall they come out with great substance." The witnesses note the promise to judge the oppressing nation and bring out the seed with substance. On "that nation, whom they shall serve, will I judge," the grammatical voices note the judgment. Gill and Calvin note that "also that nation, whom they shall serve, will I judge" — God promised to judge (punish) the nation (Egypt) that would enslave and afflict Abram's seed; the judgment on Egypt (the plagues, Exodus 7-12); God's justice on the oppressors; the assurance that the bondage would end in the judgment of the enslaving nation.

On "will I judge," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note God's justice. Calvin and Gill note that God himself would judge Egypt — the oppressor of his people; the divine vengeance on those who afflicted the chosen seed; the comfort of the promise that God would not leave the bondage unavenged; the judgment that would accompany the deliverance.

On "afterward shall they come out with great substance," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the deliverance: "afterward shall they come out with great substance" — after the bondage and the judgment, Abram's seed would come out of Egypt with great wealth (the spoiling of the Egyptians, Exodus 12:35-36); the deliverance and enrichment of Israel; the promise that the affliction would end in liberation and abundance; the bondage turned to blessing by God's faithfulness. The verse displays the promise to judge the oppressor and bring out the seed with substance.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the judgment — "that nation, whom they shall serve, will I judge," God's judgment on Egypt (Exodus 7-12, Gill, Calvin); God himself judging the oppressor (Calvin, Gill); "afterward shall they come out with great substance," the deliverance and enrichment of Israel (Exodus 12:35-36, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promised deliverance. The verse marks the nation: judged. The verse displays the Egypt: punished. The verse displays the seed: brought out. The verse displays the substance: great. The promise displays God judging the oppressor and freeing the seed. The verse displays the judgment: on Egypt. The verse displays the deliverance: with wealth. The verse displays the faithfulness: of God. The promise to judge the oppressor and bring out the seed with substance displays the assured deliverance: I will judge that nation, and afterward they shall come out with great substance — God's judgment on Egypt (Exodus 7-12) and the deliverance of Israel with great wealth (Exodus 12:35-36), the bondage ending in liberation. The verse displays the promised deliverance, with Abram's peaceful death (v.15).
15:
"And thou shalt go to thy fathers in peace; thou shalt be buried in a good old age." The witnesses note the promise of Abram's peaceful death and burial in a good old age. On "thou shalt go to thy fathers in peace," the grammatical voices note the death. Ellicott and Gill note that "thou shalt go to thy fathers in peace" — God promised Abram a peaceful death; "go to thy fathers" implies a gathering to his ancestors, and (as some note) a continued existence beyond death (the soul gathered to the fathers, not merely the body buried); the patriarch would die in peace, before the affliction of his seed.

On "go to thy fathers in peace," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the hope. Calvin and Gill note that "go to thy fathers" suggests the gathering of Abram to his people beyond death; the phrase implies a hope of immortality (the fathers being not annihilated but gathered); Abram would die in peace, in the favor and hope of God; the peaceful end of the man of faith.

On "buried in a good old age," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the long life: "buried in a good old age" — Abram would live to a great age and be honorably buried (fulfilled, 25:7-8, Abram dying at 175, buried by Isaac and Ishmael); the promise of a long, full life and a peaceful death; the blessing of the patriarch, who would not see the affliction of his seed but die in peace and hope. The verse displays the promise of Abram's peaceful death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the death — "thou shalt go to thy fathers in peace," Abram's peaceful death, gathered to his fathers (Ellicott, Gill); "go to thy fathers," the gathering beyond death, a hope of immortality (Calvin, Gill); "buried in a good old age," the long life and honorable burial (25:7-8, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promised peace. The verse marks the death: in peace. The verse displays the fathers: gathered to. The verse displays the old age: good. The verse displays the burial: honorable. The promise displays Abram's peaceful end in a good old age. The verse displays the Abram: in peace. The verse displays the age: great. The verse displays the hope: beyond death. The promise of Abram's peaceful death and burial in a good old age displays the blessing of the man of faith: thou shalt go to thy fathers in peace and be buried in a good old age — the patriarch's peaceful death (25:7-8), gathered to his fathers in the hope beyond death, before the affliction of his seed. The verse displays the promised peace, with the iniquity of the Amorites (v.16).
16:
"But in the fourth generation they shall come hither again: for the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet full." The witnesses note the return in the fourth generation and the iniquity of the Amorites not yet full — the crux of God's forbearance and justice. On "in the fourth generation they shall come hither again," the grammatical voices note the return. Ellicott and Gill note that "in the fourth generation they shall come hither again" — Abram's seed, after the bondage (v.13), would return to Canaan in the fourth generation; "the fourth generation" is variously reckoned (four generations of the long-lived patriarchs, corresponding to the four hundred years, v.13; or the fourth generation of those who went down to Egypt); the return of Israel to the land under Joshua.

On "for the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet full," interpreters expound the moral ground. The reformed Calvin and the grammatical-historical note God's forbearance and justice: "the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet full" — the Amorites (the Canaanite nations) were not yet so wicked as to be ripe for the judgment of dispossession; God, in his justice, would not give their land to Israel until their iniquity was full (complete, deserving of the judgment); the delay of four hundred years was God's forbearance, giving the Canaanites space to repent (or to fill up the measure of their sin); when their wickedness was full, God would judge them (by Israel's conquest); the moral ground of the conquest — not Israel's merit, but the Canaanites' ripened iniquity (Deuteronomy 9:4-5; Leviticus 18:24-28); the patience and the justice of God displayed.

On the iniquity not yet full, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the patience of God: God's long forbearance with the Amorites (four hundred years) shows his patience, not willing that any should perish; yet his justice, when their sin was full, would not spare; the dispossession of the Canaanites a righteous judgment on a people ripe in wickedness; the forbearance and the severity of God. The verse displays the return in the fourth generation and the iniquity of the Amorites.

The witnesses agree the substance: Abram's seed would return in the fourth generation, for the Amorites' iniquity was not yet full (Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke). The phrase "the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet full" bears its weight as a crux of God's forbearance and justice: God delayed the conquest four hundred years, in patience giving the Canaanites space, until their iniquity was full and ripe for judgment; the moral ground of the conquest (the Canaanites' ripened wickedness, not Israel's merit, Deuteronomy 9:4-5); the patience and the justice of God held together. The verse displays the Amorites-iniquity crux. The verse marks the return: fourth generation. The verse displays the iniquity: not yet full. The verse displays the forbearance: of God. The verse displays the justice: in the conquest. The phrase displays God's patience until the Amorites' sin was full. The verse displays the Amorites: spared yet. The verse displays the judgment: deferred. The verse displays the justice: ripening. The return in the fourth generation and the iniquity of the Amorites not yet full display God's forbearance and justice: in the fourth generation they shall come again, for the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet full — God delaying the conquest in patience until the Canaanites' sin was ripe for judgment, the moral ground of the conquest (Deuteronomy 9:4-5), the patience and justice of God. The verse displays God's forbearance, sealed by the covenant-sign (v.17).
17:
"And it came to pass, that, when the sun went down, and it was dark, behold a smoking furnace, and a burning lamp that passed between those pieces." The witnesses note the smoking furnace and burning lamp passing between the pieces — the crux of the covenant-sign. On "when the sun went down, and it was dark, behold a smoking furnace, and a burning lamp," the grammatical voices note the sign. Ellicott and Gill note that "when the sun went down, and it was dark, behold a smoking furnace, and a burning lamp that passed between those pieces" — at full dark, a smoking furnace (or oven) and a burning lamp (a flaming torch) appeared and passed between the divided pieces of the sacrifice (v.10); the visible sign of God's presence; the symbols of the divine glory (the Shekinah) passing through the covenant-path.

On "a smoking furnace, and a burning lamp," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the symbols. Henry and Gill note that the smoking furnace and the burning lamp "probably represented" the presence of God; the furnace (smoke) and the lamp (fire/light) figures of the divine presence (as the pillar of cloud and fire, Exodus 13:21); some see in the furnace the affliction of the seed (the iron furnace of Egypt, Deuteronomy 4:20) and in the lamp the light of deliverance; the symbols of God passing through the pieces.

On "passed between those pieces," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the one-sided covenant: "passed between those pieces" — God (in the symbols of the furnace and lamp), and not Abram, passed between the divided pieces; in the covenant-rite, the party who passed between the pieces bound himself (invoking the like fate if he broke the covenant); here God alone passed through, binding himself by oath to Abram; the covenant of grace, in which God alone is the covenant-maker and surety; God pledging himself, unconditionally, to fulfill the promise; the wonder that God, condescending to the human form of covenant, bound himself with an oath (Hebrews 6:13-18). The verse displays the smoking furnace and burning lamp passing between the pieces.

The witnesses agree the substance: at dark, the smoking furnace and burning lamp (the symbols of God's presence) passed between the divided pieces, and God made the covenant (Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin). The covenant-sign bears its weight as a crux: the furnace and lamp represent the divine presence (the Shekinah; the furnace perhaps the affliction of Egypt, the lamp the deliverance); and God alone (not Abram) passing between the pieces signifies the one-sided covenant of grace, in which God binds himself by oath to fulfill the promise (Hebrews 6:13-18). The verse displays the covenant-sign crux. The verse marks the furnace: smoking. The verse displays the lamp: burning. The verse displays the pieces: passed between. The verse displays the covenant: God binding himself. The sign displays God alone passing through, pledging himself. The verse displays the presence: divine. The verse displays the covenant: of grace. The verse displays the oath: God's. The smoking furnace and burning lamp passing between the pieces display the covenant-sign and a crux: the smoking furnace and burning lamp passed between the pieces — the symbols of God's presence (the Shekinah; the furnace the affliction, the lamp the deliverance), God alone passing through to bind himself by oath in the one-sided covenant of grace (Hebrews 6:13-18). The verse displays the covenant made, the land granted (v.18).
18:
"In the same day the LORD made a covenant with Abram, saying, Unto thy seed have I given this land, from the river of Egypt unto the great river, the river Euphrates:" The witnesses note the LORD's covenant granting the land from the river of Egypt to the Euphrates — the crux of the land's extent. On "in the same day the LORD made a covenant with Abram," the grammatical voices note the covenant. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "in the same day the LORD made a covenant with Abram" — by the sign of the furnace and lamp (v.17), the LORD solemnly made (cut) a covenant with Abram; the covenant of grant, confirming the promise of the land to Abram's seed; the formal, sworn covenant.

On "Unto thy seed have I given this land," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the grant. Calvin and Gill note that "Unto thy seed have I given this land" — God granted the land of Canaan to Abram's seed; the past tense ("have I given") marks the certainty of the gift, as good as done; the land-promise sealed by covenant; the inheritance assured to Abram's descendants.

On "from the river of Egypt unto the great river, the river Euphrates," interpreters expound the boundaries. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the extent: the land granted reached "from the river of Egypt unto the great river, the river Euphrates" — the boundaries of the grant. The "river of Egypt" is variously identified — the Nile (so some), or (more commonly, Clarke) the brook/wadi of Egypt (the Wadi el-Arish, the southern border, not the Nile). The extent (from the southern border to the Euphrates) is read by some as the ideal boundaries, fulfilled at the height of Israel's kingdom under David and Solomon (1 Kings 4:21, 24; 2 Chronicles 9:26, who reigned to the Euphrates); others note that Israel never fully or permanently possessed the whole; the covenant-grant of the land, in its widest extent, to Abram's seed. The verse displays the covenant granting the land.

The witnesses agree the substance: the LORD made a covenant granting the land to Abram's seed, from the river of Egypt to the Euphrates (Keil, Gill, Calvin, Clarke). The extent bears a crux: the "river of Egypt" the Nile vs the brook/wadi of Egypt (Wadi el-Arish, the southern border — Clarke); and the boundaries (to the Euphrates) the ideal extent, fulfilled under David and Solomon (1 Kings 4:21) vs an extent never fully or permanently possessed. The verse displays the land-extent crux. The verse marks the covenant: made. The verse displays the grant: the land. The verse displays the debate: the Nile or the wadi; the extent. The verse displays the Euphrates: the limit. The covenant displays God granting the land to Abram's seed. The verse displays the land: given. The verse displays the boundaries: marked. The verse displays the covenant: sealed. The LORD's covenant granting the land displays the sealed promise and a crux: the LORD covenanted, Unto thy seed have I given this land, from the river of Egypt to the Euphrates — the boundaries read with the "river of Egypt" as the Nile or the Wadi el-Arish, and the extent as the ideal fulfilled under David and Solomon (1 Kings 4:21) or never fully possessed. The verse displays the covenant land, inhabited by the ten nations (vv.19-21).
19:
"The Kenites, and the Kenizzites, and the Kadmonites," The very brief verse begins the list of the nations whose land was granted to Abram's seed. The grammatical voices note the peoples. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the Kenites, and the Kenizzites, and the Kadmonites" — the first three of the ten nations (vv.19-21) inhabiting the land granted to Abram's seed; the peoples whose territory was promised to Israel; the Kenites (a people later associated with Israel, the family of Jethro, Judges 1:16), the Kenizzites, and the Kadmonites (eastern peoples); the nations to be dispossessed.

On the nations, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the list. Clarke and Ellicott note that ten nations are named (vv.19-21) — more than the usual seven (Deuteronomy 7:1); the fuller list marking the wide extent of the grant (to the Euphrates, v.18), embracing peoples beyond the borders of Canaan proper; the nations whose land was given to Abram's seed.

On the Kenites and the others, the grammatical-historical note the grant: the land of these nations was granted by covenant to Abram's seed; the peoples whose territory Israel would (in part) possess; the wide extent of the promised inheritance; the nations of the land, named as the boundaries of the grant. The verse displays the beginning of the list of the nations.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the peoples — "the Kenites, and the Kenizzites, and the Kadmonites," the first three of the ten nations (Keil, Gill); the fuller list of ten, marking the wide extent (Deuteronomy 7:1, Clarke, Ellicott); the nations whose land was granted to Abram's seed (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the listed nations. The verse marks the Kenites: and Kenizzites. The verse displays the Kadmonites: named. The verse displays the nations: ten. The verse displays the grant: their land. The list displays the peoples of the promised land. The verse displays the nations: named. The verse displays the land: granted. The verse displays the extent: wide. The beginning of the list of the nations displays the extent of the grant: the Kenites, the Kenizzites, and the Kadmonites — the first of the ten nations whose land was granted by covenant to Abram's seed, the wide extent of the promised inheritance. The verse displays the listed nations, continued (v.20).
20:
"And the Hittites, and the Perizzites, and the Rephaims," The very brief verse continues the list of the nations of the land. The grammatical voices note the peoples. Gill notes that "the Hittites, and the Perizzites, and the Rephaims" — three more of the ten nations (vv.19-21) inhabiting the land granted to Abram's seed; the Hittites (the children of Heth, 10:15; 23:3), the Perizzites (a Canaanite people, 13:7), and the Rephaim (the giants, 14:5; Deuteronomy 2:11); the peoples whose land was promised to Israel.

On the Hittites and Perizzites, the witnesses note the Canaanite nations. Gill notes that the Hittites and Perizzites were among the chief Canaanite nations (often named in the lists of the peoples Israel would dispossess, e.g. 13:7; Exodus 3:8); the inhabitants of the land of promise; the nations to be driven out.

On "the Rephaims," the grammatical-historical note the giants: "the Rephaims" — the Rephaim, the ancient giant-people (14:5; Deuteronomy 3:11, Og king of Bashan of the remnant of the Rephaim); the powerful, gigantic inhabitants of parts of the land; named among the nations whose territory was granted to Abram's seed; the wide extent of the grant embracing even the lands of the giants. The verse continues the list of the nations of the land.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the peoples — "the Hittites, and the Perizzites, and the Rephaims," three more of the ten nations (Gill); the Hittites and Perizzites, chief Canaanite nations (13:7, Gill); "the Rephaims," the giant-people (14:5; Deuteronomy 3:11, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the listed nations. The verse marks the Hittites: and Perizzites. The verse displays the Rephaim: giants. The verse displays the nations: continued. The verse displays the grant: their land. The list displays more of the peoples of the promised land. The verse displays the nations: named. The verse displays the giants: included. The verse displays the land: granted. The list of the nations of the land continued displays the extent of the grant: the Hittites, the Perizzites, and the Rephaim — three more of the nations, including the giants, whose land was granted to Abram's seed. The verse continues the listed nations (v.21).
21:
"And the Amorites, and the Canaanites, and the Girgashites, and the Jebusites." The very brief verse completes the list of the nations whose land was granted to Abram's seed. The grammatical voices note the peoples. Gill notes that "the Amorites, and the Canaanites, and the Girgashites, and the Jebusites" — the last four of the ten nations (vv.19-21) inhabiting the land granted to Abram's seed; the Amorites (a chief Canaanite people, whose iniquity was not yet full, v.16), the Canaanites (the people of the land generally, or a particular tribe), the Girgashites, and the Jebusites (the people of Jebus/Jerusalem); the nations to be dispossessed by Israel.

On "the Amorites," the witnesses note the chief people. Gill notes that the Amorites (named in v.16, whose iniquity would in time be full) were a chief and powerful Canaanite nation, sometimes standing for the Canaanites generally; the people whose ripened wickedness would bring God's judgment by Israel's conquest.

On the completing of the list, the grammatical-historical note the ten nations: the ten nations (vv.19-21) — Kenites, Kenizzites, Kadmonites, Hittites, Perizzites, Rephaim, Amorites, Canaanites, Girgashites, Jebusites — are the peoples whose land was granted by covenant to Abram's seed; the full extent of the promised inheritance; the nations Israel would (in part) dispossess; the covenant of the land sealed with the naming of its inhabitants. The verse completes the list of the nations.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the peoples — "the Amorites, and the Canaanites, and the Girgashites, and the Jebusites," the last four of the ten nations (Gill); "the Amorites," the chief people, whose iniquity would be full (v.16, Gill); the ten nations whose land was granted to Abram's seed (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the completed list. The verse marks the Amorites: and Canaanites. The verse displays the Jebusites: of Jerusalem. The verse displays the nations: ten. The verse displays the grant: complete. The list displays all the peoples of the promised land. The verse displays the nations: named. The verse displays the land: granted. The verse displays the covenant: sealed. The list of the nations whose land was granted to Abram's seed completed displays the full extent of the covenant-grant: the Amorites, Canaanites, Girgashites, and Jebusites — the last of the ten nations whose land was granted by covenant to Abram's seed, the full inheritance of the promise. The verse closes the covenant chapter, before Hagar and Ishmael (ch.16).
